question:
KMP is primarily used for:
A. Exact pattern matching in a text B. Approximate string edit distance C. Suffix array construction D. Data compression E. I am not sure

KMP total complexity (preprocess + search) is:
A. O(n+m) B. O(nm) C. O(n log m) D. O(m^2) E. I am not sure

KMP preprocessing complexity for pattern length m is:
A. O(1) B. O(log m) C. O(m) D. O(m^2) E. I am not sure

KMP search phase over text length n is:
A. O(n) B. O(log n) C. O(nm) D. O(n^2) E. I am not sure

KMP extra space is mainly for prefix table of size:
A. O(1) B. O(log m) C. O(m) D. O(nm) E. I am not sure

In KMP, pi[i] stores:
A. Number of distinct chars in pattern[0..i] B. Longest proper prefix also suffix of pattern[0..i] C. First mismatch position in text D. Hash value of prefix E. I am not sure

In KMP prefix-function convention, pi[0] equals:
A. 1 B. 0 C. -1 D. pattern length E. I am not sure

On mismatch at pattern index j>0 during search, KMP sets j to:
A. j-1 B. 0 always C. pi[j-1] D. i+1 E. I am not sure

On mismatch when j==0, KMP search does:
A. i++ in text B. j-- C. Reset i to 0 D. Stop immediately E. I am not sure

During KMP search, text pointer i is:
A. Frequently moved backward B. Never decremented C. Reset after every mismatch D. Changed only on matches E. I am not sure

After one full match (j==m), to allow overlaps KMP sets:
A. j=0 always and skips one text char B. j=pi[m-1] C. j=m-1 and i-- D. j remains m forever E. I am not sure

KMP can report:
A. Only the first occurrence B. All occurrences including overlaps C. Only non-overlapping occurrences D. Only if pattern is unique E. I am not sure

KMP failure/prefix table depends on:
A. Text only B. Pattern only C. Both text and pattern lengths equally D. Alphabet frequencies in text only E. I am not sure

KMP speeds up over naive search mainly by:
A. Skipping text backtracking using border information B. Using random hashing C. Sorting text windows D. Building suffix tree of text always E. I am not sure

In KMP, pi array for pattern "ABCDE" is:
A. [0,0,0,0,0] B. [0,1,2,3,4] C. [0,0,1,2,3] D. [1,1,1,1,1] E. I am not sure

KMP is primarily used for:
A. exact string pattern matching B. all-pairs shortest path C. minimum spanning tree D. data compression transforms E. I am not sure

In the same KMP search loop, on mismatch when j==0, standard action is:
A. i++ B. j = pi[j-1] C. i-- D. terminate immediately E. I am not sure

Total pointer-backtrack work during KMP prefix-function construction for length m is:
A. O(1) B. O(log m) C. O(m) D. O(m^2) E. I am not sure

A key reason KMP search is linear is that text index i is:
A. never decremented B. decremented after each mismatch C. reset to previous match start D. sorted before scanning E. I am not sure

For text length n and pattern length m, KMP total time complexity is:
A. O(n+m) B. O(nm) C. O(n log m) D. O(m^2) E. I am not sure

KMP preprocessing (building prefix/pi table) time complexity is:
A. O(m) B. O(1) C. O(log m) D. O(m^2) E. I am not sure

After one full KMP match (j==m), to continue finding overlapping matches set:
A. j = pi[m-1] B. j = 0 and force another i++ C. j = m permanently D. j = m-1 and i-- E. I am not sure

KMP automaton interpretation: state j means processed text currently ends with:
A. a permutation of j pattern chars B. the first j chars of pattern C. the last j chars of pattern D. j unrelated matches E. I am not sure

KMP extra space (excluding input text/pattern storage) is mainly:
A. O(m) B. O(1) C. O(n) D. O(nm) E. I am not sure

KMP worst-case complexity is insensitive to alphabet size in terms of:
A. O(n+m) bound B. Memory becoming O(alphabet) C. Need for sorting alphabet D. Mandatory hashing collisions E. I am not sure
How would you efficiently search for a pattern in a text while ensuring the text pointer never moves backwards?
Which algorithm is it that precomputes a 'partial match table' for the pattern to achieve O(m+n) time complexity in string matching?
In practice, which string matching algorithm uses the concept of the longest prefix that is also a suffix (LPS) to avoid re-checking characters in the main text?
Could you explain the method that improves upon brute-force matching by preprocessing the pattern to create a 'next' array for pointer jumps?
In practice, what is the algorithm for finding all occurrences of a pattern in a text that has linear time complexity and does not backtrack the text index?
Can you describe the string search technique that utilizes a failure function derived from the pattern itself to skip unnecessary comparisons?
What algorithm would you use to implement a pattern matching algorithm where the main string is traversed only once, but the pattern pointer resets based on a precomputed table?
In practice, what algorithm, often contrasted with the Boyer-Moore algorithm, is particularly efficient for matching patterns with repetitive prefixes and suffixes?
In practice, which method solves the string matching problem by first analyzing the pattern to find overlaps within itself, then using that data during the search?
In practice, in the context of text processing, what is the name of the linear-time algorithm that relies solely on a preprocessed 'prefix table' of the pattern?
How would you avoid the O(m*n) complexity of naive string search? Describe the algorithm that preprocesses the pattern in O(m) time?
In practice, what is the principle behind the string matching algorithm that allows the pattern to slide more than one position when a mismatch occurs, based on self-similarity?
Can you describe an efficient single-pattern search algorithm commonly used in compilers for keyword recognition and in bioinformatics for DNA sequencing?
In practice, which string search algorithm is known for its 'no backtracking on the text' property and uses a state transition table derived from the pattern?
Which algorithm is it that achieves efficient string matching by exploiting the fact that a mismatch contains information to determine the next start?
